region_of_origin: India (Kerala); widely cultivated across Southeast Asia

ontology.foodex2_description: Black pepper, ground — FoodEx2 spices and herbs category

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground black pepper is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.3 g), used as a seasoning sprinkled over dishes or incorporated into recipes.

nutrient_source.title: Black pepper, ground — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for ground black pepper (cr_id F10060008). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for spices, pepper, black (NDB No. 02030) for plausibility; HPB values are consistent with USDA reference ranges.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of spices and condiments — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate spice category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ground black pepper exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on the food's very high dietary fibre content (25.3 g/100g), low sugar (0.6 g/100g), and the established principle that high-fibre, low-sugar spices have minimal glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per serving (2.3 g) = (25 × 0.89 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 0.2, confirming negligible glycaemic impact at culinary doses. Analogous low-GI spice category referenced from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (20 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (1329 mg/100g) with a strongly favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Ground black pepper is an excellent low-sodium seasoning for hypertensive individuals; its high potassium content actively supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation, and it can be used freely as a salt substitute in cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high dietary fibre (25.3 g/100g) and negligible sugar (0.6 g/100g) with estimated low GI (~25) and near-zero glycaemic load at culinary serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground black pepper poses no meaningful glycaemic risk for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; used in typical culinary quantities (1–2 g per serving), its glycaemic load is negligible, and piperine may enhance insulin sensitivity according to emerging research.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.3 g/100g), low saturated fat (~1.4 g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and high dietary fibre (25.3 g/100g) with anti-inflammatory piperine content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground black pepper is cardioprotective at culinary doses; its low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high fibre, and piperine-mediated anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties support cardiovascular health when used as a regular seasoning.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Piperine — a bioactive alkaloid with documented anti-inflammatory and cytokine-inhibiting properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black pepper's active compound piperine inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokines and enhances bioavailability of anti-inflammatory curcumin; regular culinary use is appropriate and potentially beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground black pepper is the finely milled powder of dried ripe or unripe berries of Piper nigrum, one of the world's most widely used spices. The drying and grinding process concentrates the volatile oils and the alkaloid piperine, which is responsible for the characteristic sharp, pungent, and mildly spicy flavour. The powder is dark grey-brown to near-black in colour with a fine, dry texture. It is used universally as a table condiment and culinary seasoning across virtually all global cuisines, including Singaporean, Indian, Chinese, Western, and Southeast Asian cooking. Nutritionally, ground black pepper is notable for its exceptionally high dietary fibre content (25.3 g/100g), very high potassium (1329 mg/100g), significant calcium (443 mg/100g), and high iron (9.71 mg/100g), though these values are nutritionally meaningful only in aggregate dietary context given the small culinary serving sizes typically used (1–5 g). Sodium is very low at 20 mg/100g, making it an ideal seasoning for sodium-restricted diets. Piperine, the primary bioactive compound, has well-documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and bioavailability-enhancing properties — most notably increasing the absorption of curcumin from turmeric by up to 2000%. Clinically, ground black pepper is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Its estimated glycaemic index is low (~25) and glycaemic load per typical serving is negligible (<0.5). It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10060008, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for spices, pepper, black. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed basis. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: Saturated fat (1.39 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.74 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.99 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 02030) as HPB source does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; these are flagged as adapted values. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this item; USDA SR Legacy does contain some of these values but they are not incorporated here to maintain source consistency with the primary HPB record. The dietary_tags 'wholegrain' from the input has been reviewed: ground black pepper is not a grain product and this tag is not carried forward. Tags 'low_sodium' and 'high_fibre' are retained as accurate. GI of ~25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ground black pepper exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
